Relevance for your projects: if FolioTrack needed real-time portfolio analytics without a separate reporting DB, adding columnstore indexes to the MySQL analytical queries would be the HTAP approach.

questions.tip: Key distinction for interviews: OLTP = many small fast transactions, normalised schema, optimise writes. OLAP = few large complex queries, denormalised star schema, optimise reads. Your day-to-day application databases are OLTP. Reporting/BI databases are OLAP. A data warehouse is an OLAP system fed by ETL from OLTP systems.

questions.question: What is index fragmentation in SQL Server and how do you fix it?
questions.answer:
Index fragmentation occurs when the logical order of index pages no longer matches their physical order on disk, or when index pages are not filled efficiently. It degrades query performance because SQL Server must perform extra I/O and cannot scan pages sequentially.

Two types of fragmentation:

  • Internal fragmentation (page density) — index pages have too much free space (many rows deleted). Pages are not full → more pages must be read to get the same data.
  • External fragmentation (logical fragmentation) — logical page ordering doesn't match physical ordering on disk. Read-ahead (sequential I/O) is broken → random I/O, slower scans.
-- Check fragmentation levels
SELECT
    i.name AS IndexName,
    ips.avg_fragmentation_in_percent AS FragmentationPct,
    ips.page_count AS PageCount,
    ips.avg_page_space_used_in_percent AS PageDensityPct
FROM sys.dm_db_index_physical_stats(
    DB_ID(), OBJECT_ID('dbo.UserProgress'), NULL, NULL, 'SAMPLED') ips
JOIN sys.indexes i ON i.object_id = ips.object_id
                   AND i.index_id = ips.index_id
WHERE ips.page_count > 100  -- ignore tiny indexes
ORDER BY ips.avg_fragmentation_in_percent DESC;

-- Fix fragmentation based on severity:

-- < 5% fragmentation: do nothing — not worth the overhead

-- 5–30% fragmentation: REORGANIZE (online, minimal locking)
-- Compacts pages in-place, can be interrupted and resumed
ALTER INDEX IX_UserProgress_UserId ON dbo.UserProgress REORGANIZE;

-- > 30% fragmentation: REBUILD (can be online in Enterprise Ed)
-- Drops and recreates the index — fully defragmented, updates statistics
ALTER INDEX IX_UserProgress_UserId ON dbo.UserProgress
REBUILD WITH (ONLINE = ON, FILLFACTOR = 80);

-- Rebuild ALL indexes on a table
ALTER INDEX ALL ON dbo.UserProgress REBUILD;

followUps.question: What is Fill Factor and how does it relate to fragmentation?
followUps.answer: Fill Factor specifies the percentage of space to leave free on each index leaf page when the index is built or rebuilt. Default = 100% (no free space left). When new rows are inserted that sort between existing values, SQL Server must split the full page into two pages — a page split. Page splits are expensive (writes two pages instead of one) and cause external fragmentation immediately. Setting Fill Factor to 80 means each page is 80% full at rebuild time, leaving 20% free for inserts before a split occurs. Trade-off: lower fill factor = more free space = fewer page splits = less future fragmentation, but also means more pages to read for table scans (more I/O). Rule of thumb: high Fill Factor (90–100%) for read-heavy or append-only tables. Lower Fill Factor (70–85%) for tables with frequent inserts/updates in the middle of the key range. The default Fill Factor for SQL Server is 0 (treated as 100 — full pages). Set at the server level or per-index at rebuild time.

followUps.question: How do you automate index maintenance in SQL Server?
followUps.answer: Common approaches: (1) SQL Server Agent job — schedule a nightly/weekly T-SQL job that checks sys.dm_db_index_physical_stats and applies REORGANIZE or REBUILD based on thresholds. A well-known free script: Ola Hallengren's IndexOptimize (widely used in production). (2) Adaptive Index Defrag — another free script that tracks fragmentation history and only rebuilds when improvement is expected. (3) Azure SQL Automatic Tuning — Azure SQL Database has built-in auto-index maintenance as part of its Intelligent Query Processing. (4) Maintenance Plans in SSMS — GUI wizard to create scheduled index maintenance. Simple but less flexible. Consideration: don't rebuild all indexes blindly every night — rebuild has significant I/O impact. Use thresholds (5–30% → reorganize, >30% → rebuild) and skip small indexes (< 1000 pages). In production, schedule during low-traffic windows (e.g., 2–4 AM) or use ONLINE = ON for business hours with Enterprise Edition.

questions.tip: Thresholds to remember: < 5% → do nothing. 5–30% → REORGANIZE (online, low impact). > 30% → REBUILD (higher impact, fully defragments). Check fragmentation with sys.dm_db_index_physical_stats. Fill Factor leaves intentional free space to delay future fragmentation — lower for write-heavy, higher for read-heavy tables.

questions.question: How do you delete duplicate rows in SQL Server while keeping one copy?
questions.answer:
The cleanest approach uses ROW_NUMBER() inside a CTE to identify and delete duplicates in a single statement.

Method 1: CTE + ROW_NUMBER() (recommended)

-- Keep the row with the lowest Id; delete all others with the same (FirstName, LastName, Email)
WITH DuplicateCTE AS
(
    SELECT *,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
               PARTITION BY FirstName, LastName, Email  -- define what 'duplicate' means
               ORDER BY Id                              -- which copy to keep (lowest Id)
           ) AS RowNum
    FROM Employees
)
DELETE FROM DuplicateCTE
WHERE RowNum > 1;  -- delete all rows except the first (RowNum = 1)

You can preview what will be deleted by changing DELETE FROM to SELECT * first.

Method 2: Self-join (older approach)

DELETE e1
FROM Employees e1
INNER JOIN Employees e2
    ON  e1.FirstName = e2.FirstName
    AND e1.LastName  = e2.LastName
    AND e1.Email     = e2.Email
    AND e1.Id > e2.Id;  -- keep the row with the lower Id

Method 3: Using a temp table (safest for large tables)

-- Step 1: Copy distinct rows to temp table
SELECT DISTINCT * INTO #TempClean FROM Employees;

-- Step 2: Clear original table
TRUNCATE TABLE Employees;

-- Step 3: Reinsert clean data
INSERT INTO Employees SELECT * FROM #TempClean;

DROP TABLE #TempClean;

Always run inside a transaction and test with a SELECT before committing the DELETE.

followUps.question: How do you find duplicates without deleting them?
followUps.answer:
-- Find all duplicate groups and how many times each occurs
SELECT FirstName, LastName, Email, COUNT(*) AS DuplicateCount
FROM Employees
GROUP BY FirstName, LastName, Email
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY DuplicateCount DESC;

-- See all individual duplicate rows
SELECT *
FROM Employees
WHERE Email IN (
    SELECT Email FROM Employees
    GROUP BY Email
    HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
)
ORDER BY Email, Id;

questions.tip: Always preview first: Replace DELETE with SELECT * in the CTE method to see exactly which rows will be removed. Wrap in a transaction with ROLLBACK until you're confident.

questions.question: How do you optimise a slow SQL query or stored procedure?
questions.answer:
Query optimisation is a systematic process. Here's the approach I follow:

Step 1: Check the execution plan

In SSMS: press Ctrl+M to enable Actual Execution Plan, then run the query. Look for:

  • Table Scan / Clustered Index Scan — reading the whole table, no index used. Usually the biggest problem.
  • Index Seek — good, using an index efficiently.
  • Hash Match / Sort with high cost% — may indicate missing indexes or sorting large datasets.
  • Key Lookup — occurs when a non-clustered index is used but extra columns are needed from the base table. Fix with covering indexes (INCLUDE columns).

Step 2: Add appropriate indexes

-- Non-clustered index on filter/join column
CREATE INDEX IX_Orders_CustomerId ON Orders (CustomerId);

-- Covering index — includes extra columns to avoid key lookups
CREATE INDEX IX_Orders_CustomerId_Status
ON Orders (CustomerId, Status)
INCLUDE (OrderDate, TotalAmount);

Step 3: Rewrite the query

  • Avoid SELECT * — select only columns you need.
  • Replace correlated subqueries with JOINs.
  • Use EXISTS instead of IN with subqueries for large sets.
  • Avoid functions on indexed columns in WHERE: WHERE YEAR(OrderDate) = 2024 prevents index use — rewrite as WHERE OrderDate >= '2024-01-01' AND OrderDate < '2025-01-01'.
  • Replace cursors with set-based operations.

Step 4: Stored procedure specific

-- Add at the top of every stored procedure
SET NOCOUNT ON;  -- suppresses 'rows affected' messages, small perf gain

-- Handle parameter sniffing with OPTION(RECOMPILE) or OPTIMIZE FOR
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE CustomerId = @CustomerId
OPTION (RECOMPILE);

-- Or use local variables to avoid sniffing
DECLARE @LocalId INT = @CustomerId;
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE CustomerId = @LocalId;

Step 5: Statistics and fragmentation

UPDATE STATISTICS Orders;        -- update stale statistics
ALTER INDEX ALL ON Orders REBUILD; -- rebuild fragmented indexes

followUps.question: What is parameter sniffing in SQL Server?
followUps.answer:
When a stored procedure is first executed, SQL Server compiles an execution plan based on the parameter values used in that first call. This plan is cached and reused for all subsequent calls — even if later calls have very different parameter values. If the first call used a parameter that returns 1 row, the plan may use a Nested Loop join. If later calls return 1 million rows, that plan is terrible — but SQL Server keeps using it. This is parameter sniffing.

Fixes: OPTION (RECOMPILE) (recompiles each execution — overhead but optimal plan each time), OPTION (OPTIMIZE FOR (@param = value)) (compile for a specific value), local variables (values unknown at compile time, SQL Server makes an average-row estimate), or WITH (RECOMPILE) on the CREATE PROCEDURE.

questions.tip: Start with the execution plan. 80% of query performance issues are solved by adding an index on the right column. Look for Table Scans first.

questions.question: What is the difference between a stored procedure and a function in SQL Server?
questions.answer:
Both are named, reusable blocks of SQL code, but they serve different purposes and have different rules.

Stored Procedure

  • Created with CREATE PROCEDURE.
  • Called with EXEC or EXECUTE.
  • Can have input and output parameters.
  • Can perform DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) and DDL (CREATE, DROP).
  • Can use transactions (BEGIN TRAN, COMMIT, ROLLBACK).
  • Can call other stored procedures.
  • Cannot be used in a SELECT statement.
  • Can return zero, one, or multiple result sets.

Function (Scalar and Table-Valued)

  • Created with CREATE FUNCTION.
  • Must return a value (scalar) or a table (TVF).
  • Called inside SQL expressions (SELECT, WHERE, JOIN).
  • Cannot perform DML (no INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE in T-SQL functions).
  • Cannot use transactions.
  • Scalar functions in WHERE clauses prevent index use — avoid for large tables.
-- Stored Procedure
CREATE PROCEDURE GetEmployeesByDept @DeptId INT
AS
BEGIN
    SET NOCOUNT ON;
    SELECT Id, Name, Salary FROM Employees WHERE DepartmentId = @DeptId;
END;

EXEC GetEmployeesByDept @DeptId = 3;

-- Scalar Function
CREATE FUNCTION dbo.GetFullName(@FirstName NVARCHAR(50), @LastName NVARCHAR(50))
RETURNS NVARCHAR(101)
AS
BEGIN
    RETURN @FirstName + ' ' + @LastName;
END;

SELECT dbo.GetFullName(FirstName, LastName) AS FullName FROM Employees;

-- Table-Valued Function (TVF)
CREATE FUNCTION dbo.GetTopEarners(@MinSalary DECIMAL(10,2))
RETURNS TABLE
AS
RETURN
    SELECT Id, Name, Salary FROM Employees WHERE Salary >= @MinSalary;

-- Use in a JOIN
SELECT e.Name, d.Name AS Dept
FROM dbo.GetTopEarners(50000) e
JOIN Departments d ON e.DepartmentId = d.Id;

followUps.question: What is the difference between a scalar function and a table-valued function (TVF)?
followUps.answer:
Scalar function returns a single value (one row, one column). Can be used anywhere a scalar expression is valid.

Table-valued function (TVF) returns a table and can be used in the FROM clause like a table or view. There are two types:

  • Inline TVF — a single RETURN SELECT statement. The query optimizer can 'inline' this into the calling query, allowing index use. Very efficient.
  • Multi-statement TVF (MSTVF) — has a BEGIN...END block and inserts rows into a declared table variable. Opaque to the optimizer — it can't look inside, leading to poor cardinality estimates. Avoid for performance-critical code.

questions.tip: Rule of thumb: If you need to encapsulate logic and reuse it in a SELECT, use a TVF. If you need to perform data modifications (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) or complex logic, use a stored procedure.

questions.question: Write a SQL query to count the number of employees in each department.
questions.answer:
This is a classic GROUP BY aggregation query — a staple of SQL interviews.

Basic query:

SELECT DepartmentId,
       COUNT(*) AS EmployeeCount
FROM Employees
GROUP BY DepartmentId
ORDER BY EmployeeCount DESC;

With department names (using JOIN):

SELECT d.Id,
       d.Name      AS DepartmentName,
       COUNT(e.Id) AS EmployeeCount
FROM Departments d
LEFT JOIN Employees e ON e.DepartmentId = d.Id
-- LEFT JOIN ensures departments with 0 employees are included
GROUP BY d.Id, d.Name
ORDER BY EmployeeCount DESC;

With only departments that have more than 5 employees (HAVING):

SELECT d.Name, COUNT(e.Id) AS EmployeeCount
FROM Departments d
INNER JOIN Employees e ON e.DepartmentId = d.Id
GROUP BY d.Id, d.Name
HAVING COUNT(e.Id) > 5
ORDER BY EmployeeCount DESC;

With additional stats:

SELECT d.Name                              AS Department,
       COUNT(e.Id)                        AS HeadCount,
       AVG(e.Salary)                      AS AvgSalary,
       MAX(e.Salary)                      AS MaxSalary,
       MIN(e.Salary)                      AS MinSalary,
       SUM(e.Salary)                      AS TotalPayroll
FROM Departments d
LEFT JOIN Employees e ON e.DepartmentId = d.Id
GROUP BY d.Id, d.Name
ORDER BY HeadCount DESC;

followUps.question: What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING in this context?
followUps.answer:
WHERE filters rows before grouping — it cannot reference aggregate functions. HAVING filters groups after grouping — it can reference aggregates like COUNT(), SUM().

-- WHERE filters individual rows before grouping
SELECT DepartmentId, COUNT(*) AS Total
FROM Employees
WHERE Salary > 30000         -- filter rows: only consider employees with salary > 30K
GROUP BY DepartmentId
HAVING COUNT(*) > 3;         -- filter groups: only departments with more than 3 such employees

Think of it as: WHERE = row-level filter, HAVING = group-level filter. You can use both in the same query.

questions.tip: Use LEFT JOIN (not INNER JOIN) when listing departments with counts — INNER JOIN would exclude departments with zero employees. If you want zero-employee departments to appear in the list, LEFT JOIN is essential.

questions.question: What is denormalization? When do you intentionally break normal form?
questions.answer:
Normalisation removes redundancy and protects data integrity. Denormalisation deliberately re-introduces some redundancy for performance. It's a trade-off I've had to make a few times on reporting-heavy systems.

Why normalisation causes performance pain:

A fully normalised schema requires JOINs to get useful data. A 5-table JOIN to display one product in a product listing page, running 10,000 times per minute, adds up. The query is correct but slow.

Common denormalisation strategies:

1. Stored/computed columns (most common)
-- Instead of computing total every time:
SELECT SUM(Quantity * UnitPrice) FROM OrderItems WHERE OrderId = 1

-- Store it directly on the order:
ALTER TABLE Orders ADD TotalAmount DECIMAL(10,2);
-- Update on every order item change
-- Trade-off: one extra update per write, zero extra work per read
2. Flattening lookup tables
-- Normalised: Orders JOIN Customers to get CustomerName
-- Denormalised: store CustomerName directly on Order
ALTER TABLE Orders ADD CustomerName NVARCHAR(100);
-- Risk: customer changes name -- Orders still show old name
-- Acceptable when: Orders are historical records (name at time of order matters)
3. Read model / CQRS projection tables

Rather than denormalising the transactional tables, create separate read-optimised tables that aggregate data. The write side stays normalised; the read side is pre-joined.

-- Read model table for product listing page
CREATE TABLE ProductListingReadModel (
    ProductId    INT,
    Name         NVARCHAR(200),
    CategoryName NVARCHAR(100), -- denormalised from Categories
    Price        DECIMAL(10,2),
    StockCount   INT,           -- denormalised from Inventory
    AverageRating DECIMAL(3,2), -- pre-computed from Reviews
    ReviewCount  INT
);
-- Rebuilt when products/categories/inventory/reviews change
-- SELECT is now a single table scan -- blazing fast

When I denormalise:

  • Reporting/analytics queries that join 5+ tables and run constantly.
  • Historical records where the value at point-in-time matters more than current value (order confirmation email, invoice line items).
  • Read-heavy endpoints where the join cost is measured and proven to be the bottleneck.
  • Data warehouses / OLAP — these are intentionally denormalised (star schema).

When I don't:

  • OLTP — transactional systems should stay normalised to preserve data integrity.
  • Before measuring — denormalisation adds complexity; prove you need it first.

followUps.question: What is the difference between OLTP and OLAP database design?
followUps.answer:
OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) — designed for fast, concurrent reads and writes of individual rows. Think: a bank processing deposits, an e-commerce site placing orders. Highly normalised (3NF or higher), many narrow tables, optimised for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE. SQL Server, PostgreSQL in transactional mode.

OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) — designed for complex analytical queries over large volumes of historical data. Think: 'What were sales by region for the last 12 months, broken down by product category?'. Intentionally denormalised — star schema or snowflake schema, wide fact tables, dimension tables. Optimised for SELECT aggregations over millions of rows. Azure Synapse, Redshift, BigQuery, Snowflake.

Most real applications have both: an OLTP database for day-to-day operations, and a data warehouse (OLAP) for reporting. ETL or CDC (Change Data Capture) moves data from OLTP to OLAP on a schedule.

questions.tip: CQRS + read models is the modern approach. Instead of denormalising your transactional tables (which creates data consistency headaches), create separate read-optimised projections. Your write model stays clean; your read model is optimised for queries.

questions.question: What is SQL Server Connection Pooling? How does it work and what are the pitfalls?
questions.answer:
Creating a new database connection is expensive — it involves network handshakes, authentication, and resource allocation on the SQL Server side. Connection pooling reuses existing connections rather than creating new ones on every request. ADO.NET handles this automatically — you don't write any pooling code yourself, but understanding it explains a lot of production issues.

How it works:

When you call conn.Open(), ADO.NET doesn't always create a new physical connection. It checks a pool of available connections with the same connection string. If one is available, it returns it. When you call conn.Close() or conn.Dispose() (via using), the connection doesn't actually close — it returns to the pool for the next caller.

// Pool settings in connection string
var connStr = new SqlConnectionStringBuilder
{
    DataSource = "server",
    InitialCatalog = "MyDb",
    IntegratedSecurity = true,
    Pooling = true,          // enabled by default
    MinPoolSize = 5,         // keep 5 connections always open
    MaxPoolSize = 100,       // max connections in pool
    ConnectTimeout = 30
}.ConnectionString;

EF Core and pooling:

When you register DbContext as Scoped (the default), EF Core creates a new DbContext per request — but that DbContext's underlying SqlConnection still uses the ADO.NET pool. There's also DbContext pooling in EF Core, which pools the DbContext object itself:

// EF Core DbContext pooling -- higher throughput for simple cases
builder.Services.AddDbContextPool<AppDbContext>(opts =>
    opts.UseSqlServer(connStr),
    poolSize: 128); // default 1024

Connection pool exhaustion — the silent killer:

This is the production issue that looks like random timeouts with the error: 'Timeout expired. The timeout period elapsed prior to obtaining a connection from the pool.'

It happens when:

  • You forget to dispose SqlConnection (no using block) — connections leak into the pool and are never returned.
  • You hold connections open during long operations (slow loops, waiting for user input).
  • Traffic spike exceeds MaxPoolSize.
  • Long-running transactions hold connections until committed.
// BAD -- connection never returned to pool
var conn = new SqlConnection(connStr);
conn.Open();
// ... forgot to close or dispose

// GOOD -- always use 'using'
using var conn = new SqlConnection(connStr);
conn.Open();
// conn.Dispose() called automatically, connection returned to pool

Diagnosing pool exhaustion:

  • Monitor sp_who2 or sys.dm_exec_sessions in SQL Server to see active connections.
  • Use Performance Counters: 'NumberOfReclaimedConnections', 'NumberOfPooledConnections'.
  • Set a lower MaxPoolSize in dev to reproduce the issue faster.

followUps.question: How does changing a connection string affect pooling?
followUps.answer: Connection pools are keyed on the exact connection string — every unique string gets its own pool. So if you dynamically build connection strings with different usernames, passwords, or options, you'll create a separate pool for each unique string. In multi-tenant apps where each tenant has its own DB user, this means N connection pools (one per tenant). That's usually fine but be aware of the pool-per-tenant overhead. If connection strings differ only by whitespace or property order, ADO.NET treats them as different strings — this is a common gotcha that leads to unexpected pool fragmentation.

questions.tip: Always use 'using' for SqlConnection. This is the single most important rule. Connection leaks are insidious — the app works fine under low load and mysteriously breaks under traffic spikes.

questions.question: What are subqueries in SQL? What is the difference between a correlated and non-correlated subquery?
questions.answer:
A subquery is a query nested inside another query. I use them regularly, but I also know when they're a performance trap — and when to replace them with a JOIN or CTE.

Non-correlated subquery — runs once, independent of outer query:

-- Find employees who earn more than the average salary
SELECT Name, Salary
FROM Employees
WHERE Salary > (SELECT AVG(Salary) FROM Employees); -- runs ONCE, result reused

-- Using IN with a subquery
SELECT Name
FROM Employees
WHERE DepartmentId IN (
    SELECT Id FROM Departments WHERE Name = 'Engineering'
);
-- Non-correlated -- inner query runs once, returns a list, outer filters by it

-- Subquery in FROM clause (derived table)
SELECT DeptName, AvgSalary
FROM (
    SELECT d.Name AS DeptName, AVG(e.Salary) AS AvgSalary
    FROM Departments d
    JOIN Employees e ON e.DepartmentId = d.Id
    GROUP BY d.Name
) AS DeptStats
WHERE AvgSalary > 60000;

Correlated subquery — runs once PER ROW of the outer query:

-- Find employees who earn more than the average for THEIR department
-- The subquery references e.DepartmentId from the OUTER query
SELECT e.Name, e.Salary, e.DepartmentId
FROM Employees e
WHERE e.Salary > (
    SELECT AVG(e2.Salary)
    FROM Employees e2
    WHERE e2.DepartmentId = e.DepartmentId -- references outer query's row
);
-- This runs the subquery ONCE PER EMPLOYEE ROW -- can be slow on large tables

-- EXISTS with correlated subquery (very common pattern)
SELECT c.Name
FROM Customers c
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM Orders o
    WHERE o.CustomerId = c.Id  -- correlated -- checks per customer
    AND o.OrderDate >= '2024-01-01'
);
-- Returns customers who have placed an order since Jan 2024
-- EXISTS stops at the first match -- faster than IN for large sets

EXISTS vs IN — important distinction:

-- IN -- problematic with NULLs and large subquery results
SELECT * FROM Employees
WHERE DepartmentId IN (SELECT Id FROM Departments WHERE IsActive = 1);
-- If any Id in the subquery is NULL, IN returns empty result (NULL logic)

-- EXISTS -- preferred for correlated checks, handles NULLs correctly
SELECT * FROM Employees e
WHERE EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM Departments d
    WHERE d.Id = e.DepartmentId AND d.IsActive = 1
);
-- Short-circuits at first match, no NULL issues

When to use subquery vs JOIN vs CTE:

  • Subquery — good for scalar values (single result), EXISTS checks, derived tables used once.
  • JOIN — better performance than correlated subqueries for most cases, especially when you need columns from both tables.
  • CTE — best for readability when subquery would be nested 2+ levels deep, or when the same subquery is referenced multiple times.

followUps.question: Why can correlated subqueries be a performance problem?
followUps.answer:
A correlated subquery executes once for every row in the outer query. If the outer query returns 10,000 rows, the subquery runs 10,000 times. For large tables, this can turn a 1-second query into a 100-second one.

Fix — rewrite as a JOIN:

-- Slow correlated subquery (runs once per employee)
SELECT Name, Salary
FROM Employees e
WHERE Salary > (SELECT AVG(Salary) FROM Employees e2 WHERE e2.DeptId = e.DeptId);

-- Fast equivalent using CTE + JOIN
WITH DeptAvg AS (
    SELECT DeptId, AVG(Salary) AS AvgSalary
    FROM Employees
    GROUP BY DeptId  -- runs ONCE
)
SELECT e.Name, e.Salary
FROM Employees e
JOIN DeptAvg d ON e.DeptId = d.DeptId
WHERE e.Salary > d.AvgSalary;

Always check the execution plan when you have correlated subqueries in production queries — look for loops/nested loops with high estimated rows.

questions.tip: EXISTS over IN for safety. IN with a subquery that can return NULLs will silently return zero rows — a hard bug to track down. EXISTS never has this issue. Also, EXISTS short-circuits at the first match, making it faster for large sets.

questions.question: What are constraints in SQL? Explain all types with examples.
questions.answer:
Constraints are rules enforced at the database level to ensure data integrity. They're your last line of defence — even if application code has a bug, constraints prevent bad data from entering the database. I always define constraints in migrations or schema scripts, not just in application validation.

1. PRIMARY KEY

-- Uniquely identifies each row, NOT NULL, one per table
CREATE TABLE Orders (
    Id INT PRIMARY KEY IDENTITY(1,1), -- auto-increment
    CustomerId INT NOT NULL
);

-- Composite primary key
CREATE TABLE OrderItems (
    OrderId   INT,
    ProductId INT,
    Quantity  INT,
    PRIMARY KEY (OrderId, ProductId) -- combination must be unique
);

2. FOREIGN KEY

-- Enforces referential integrity between tables
CREATE TABLE Orders (
    Id         INT PRIMARY KEY,
    CustomerId INT NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (CustomerId) REFERENCES Customers(Id)
        ON DELETE CASCADE    -- delete order when customer deleted
        ON UPDATE CASCADE    -- update FK when PK changes
);
-- Can't insert an Order with a CustomerId that doesn't exist in Customers
-- Can't delete a Customer who has Orders (without CASCADE)

3. UNIQUE

-- Column value must be unique across the table (NULLs allowed)
CREATE TABLE Users (
    Id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Email NVARCHAR(200) UNIQUE,  -- no two users with same email
    Phone NVARCHAR(20)  UNIQUE   -- no two users with same phone
);

-- Composite unique constraint
ALTER TABLE Products
ADD CONSTRAINT UQ_Products_NameCategory UNIQUE (Name, CategoryId);
-- Same product name allowed in different categories, not in the same category

4. NOT NULL

CREATE TABLE Employees (
    Id        INT PRIMARY KEY,
    FirstName NVARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,  -- must always have a value
    LastName  NVARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    MiddleName NVARCHAR(50) NULL      -- optional (NULL = 'no value')
);

5. CHECK

-- Validates values against a condition
CREATE TABLE Products (
    Id       INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Name     NVARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    Price    DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (Price >= 0),           -- no negative prices
    Stock    INT           CHECK (Stock >= 0),           -- no negative stock
    Rating   DECIMAL(2,1)  CHECK (Rating BETWEEN 0 AND 5),
    Status   NVARCHAR(20)  CHECK (Status IN ('Active', 'Inactive', 'Discontinued'))
);

6. DEFAULT

-- Provides a default value when no value is specified
CREATE TABLE Orders (
    Id         INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Status     NVARCHAR(20)   DEFAULT 'Pending',
    CreatedAt  DATETIME2      DEFAULT GETUTCDATE(),
    IsDeleted  BIT            DEFAULT 0
);

-- Using default
INSERT INTO Orders (Id) VALUES (1);
-- Status = 'Pending', CreatedAt = current UTC time, IsDeleted = 0

Adding constraints to existing tables:

-- Named constraint (better -- you can reference it later to drop)
ALTER TABLE Employees
ADD CONSTRAINT CHK_Salary CHECK (Salary > 0),
ADD CONSTRAINT UQ_Employees_Email UNIQUE (Email);

-- Drop a named constraint
ALTER TABLE Employees DROP CONSTRAINT CHK_Salary;

followUps.question: What is the difference between PRIMARY KEY and UNIQUE constraint?
followUps.answer:
Both enforce uniqueness, but with important differences:

  • PRIMARY KEY — one per table, no NULLs allowed, always creates a clustered index (by default in SQL Server).
  • UNIQUE — multiple per table, allows one NULL (in SQL Server — multiple NULLs in PostgreSQL), creates a non-clustered index by default.

Example: Employee table — Id is PRIMARY KEY (identity, never null). Email is UNIQUE (must be distinct, but an unregistered user might not have one yet — though I'd argue email should be NOT NULL too). NationalInsuranceNumber is UNIQUE NOT NULL (every employee has one, each is unique, but it's not the system identifier).

questions.tip: Name all your constraints. AUTO-named constraints (SQL Server generates names like 'DF__Orders__Status__3B75D760') are hard to drop and manage in migrations. Always give them explicit names: CHK_Price, UQ_Email, FK_Orders_Customers. Your future self will thank you.

questions.question: What are the types of database relationships? Explain one-to-one, one-to-many, and many-to-many.
questions.answer:
Database relationships define how tables relate to each other. There are three fundamental types:

1. One-to-One (1:1)

Each row in Table A maps to exactly one row in Table B. Used when you want to split a large table or store optional data separately.

-- Person and Passport (a person has at most one passport)
CREATE TABLE Person (
    PersonId INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Name NVARCHAR(100)
);

CREATE TABLE Passport (
    PassportId INT PRIMARY KEY,
    PersonId INT UNIQUE,  -- UNIQUE enforces 1:1
    PassportNumber NVARCHAR(50),
    FOREIGN KEY (PersonId) REFERENCES Person(PersonId)
);

2. One-to-Many (1:N)

The most common relationship. One row in Table A can have many rows in Table B. The foreign key goes on the "many" side.

-- Customer and Orders (one customer, many orders)
CREATE TABLE Customer (
    CustomerId INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Name NVARCHAR(100)
);

CREATE TABLE Orders (
    OrderId INT PRIMARY KEY,
    CustomerId INT,  -- FK on the many side
    OrderDate DATE,
    FOREIGN KEY (CustomerId) REFERENCES Customer(CustomerId)
);

3. Many-to-Many (M:N)

Requires a junction table (also called bridge or associative table) to break it into two 1:N relationships. A student can enroll in many courses; a course can have many students.

CREATE TABLE Student (StudentId INT PRIMARY KEY, Name NVARCHAR(100));
CREATE TABLE Course  (CourseId  INT PRIMARY KEY, Title NVARCHAR(100));

-- Junction table
CREATE TABLE StudentCourse (
    StudentId INT,
    CourseId  INT,
    EnrolledOn DATE,
    PRIMARY KEY (StudentId, CourseId),  -- composite PK prevents duplicates
    FOREIGN KEY (StudentId) REFERENCES Student(StudentId),
    FOREIGN KEY (CourseId)  REFERENCES Course(CourseId)
);

In EF Core, many-to-many is handled automatically with a skip navigation (student.Courses ↔ course.Students). EF Core 5+ creates the junction table implicitly, but I often define it explicitly if I need to store extra columns on the relationship (like EnrolledOn).

followUps.question: How do you implement many-to-many in EF Core?
What is a composite primary key?
When would you use a one-to-one relationship instead of putting everything in one table?

questions.tip: For many-to-many, always create a named junction entity in EF Core if the relationship has extra data.

questions.question: What is SET NOCOUNT ON/OFF in SQL Server? When should you use it?
questions.answer:
SET NOCOUNT ON suppresses the "X rows affected" messages that SQL Server sends back to the client after every INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or SELECT statement.

Why it matters in stored procedures:

CREATE PROCEDURE uspGetOrderSummary
AS
BEGIN
    SET NOCOUNT ON;  -- suppress row-count messages

    SELECT o.OrderId, o.CustomerName, SUM(oi.Amount) AS Total
    FROM Orders o
    JOIN OrderItems oi ON o.OrderId = oi.OrderId
    GROUP BY o.OrderId, o.CustomerName;
END

Benefits of SET NOCOUNT ON:

  1. Performance — reduces network traffic, especially in loops or stored procs that execute many DML statements
  2. ORM compatibility — ORMs like EF Core use row counts to validate updates. If a stored proc doesn't use SET NOCOUNT ON and returns extra result sets, it can confuse EF's optimistic concurrency check
  3. Cleaner output — when calling from application code via ADO.NET or Dapper, you don't get spurious "1 row(s) affected" messages mixed in

When NOT to use it:

If you're calling a stored proc from legacy code that checks @@ROWCOUNT or relies on the row-count messages for logic — but honestly, in modern code you'd use OUTPUT params or return values instead.

I make it a habit to always put SET NOCOUNT ON at the top of every stored procedure I write. It's a minor thing but it's considered best practice and interviewers notice it.

followUps.question: How is @@ROWCOUNT affected by SET NOCOUNT ON?
What is SET XACT_ABORT ON and when do you use it?
Does SET NOCOUNT ON improve performance significantly?

questions.tip: Always add SET NOCOUNT ON at the top of stored procedures — it's a best practice question.

questions.question: What is RAISERROR in SQL Server? How does it differ from THROW?
questions.answer:
Both RAISERROR and THROW raise errors in T-SQL, but THROW (introduced in SQL Server 2012) is the modern approach and I always use it in new code.

RAISERROR (legacy)

-- Old way
IF @UserId IS NULL
BEGIN
    RAISERROR('UserId cannot be null', 16, 1);
    -- Severity 16 = user error, State 1 = arbitrary
    RETURN;
END

-- With formatting
RAISERROR('Order %d not found for user %s', 16, 1, @OrderId, @UserName);

THROW (modern — SQL Server 2012+)

-- New way
IF @UserId IS NULL
BEGIN
    THROW 50001, 'UserId cannot be null', 1;
    -- Error number, message, state
END

-- Re-throw inside CATCH (preserves original error)
BEGIN TRY
    -- risky operation
END CATCH
    THROW;  -- re-throws with original error number/message/line
END CATCH

Key differences:

FeatureRAISERRORTHROW
SyntaxComplex with severity/stateSimpler
Re-throw originalCannot easilyTHROW; (no args) re-throws exactly
Aborts batchDepends on severityAlways aborts after THROW
SET XACT_ABORTDoesn't auto-rollbackRespects XACT_ABORT

In practice: I use THROW in all new stored procedures and wrap risky code in TRY...CATCH. I use THROW; (bare, no arguments) in CATCH blocks to re-raise the original exception back to the application layer where the actual error handling and logging happens.

followUps.question: What is TRY...CATCH in T-SQL?
What does SET XACT_ABORT ON do with THROW?
What are user-defined error numbers (50001+) in SQL Server?

questions.tip: THROW re-raises the original error cleanly — RAISERROR can't do that. Use THROW in new code.

questions.question: What is the difference between a Table Scan, Index Scan, and Index Seek in SQL Server?
questions.answer:
These three terms come up constantly when you're reading execution plans, and understanding the difference directly impacts how you optimize queries.

Table Scan

SQL Server reads every single row in the table from beginning to end. This happens when:

  • There's no useful index on the filtered columns
  • The optimizer estimates it's cheaper than an index (e.g., returning >30% of rows)
  • The table is very small (heap scan is fast on small tables)
-- This will likely cause a Table Scan if no index on LastName
SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE LastName LIKE '%smith%'
-- LIKE with leading wildcard can't use index efficiently

Index Scan

SQL Server reads all (or most) leaf-level pages of an index rather than the whole table. Still sequential, but faster than a table scan if the index is narrower than the table. It happens when:

  • An index exists but the query doesn't filter enough (returns many rows)
  • Covering index present but predicate isn't highly selective

Index Seek ✅ (What you want)

SQL Server goes directly to the specific rows matching the predicate via the B-tree. This is the most efficient operation — O(log n) instead of O(n).

-- Index Seek if there's an index on EmployeeId
SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE EmployeeId = 12345

-- Force seek hint if optimizer chooses wrong plan:
SELECT * FROM Employees WITH (INDEX(IX_EmployeeId))
WHERE EmployeeId = 12345

How to check in execution plan:

SET STATISTICS IO ON;
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE CustomerId = 100;
-- Look at "logical reads" — high count = scan, low count = seek

In practice: if I see a Table Scan or Index Scan on a large table in an execution plan, my first move is to check if an index exists on the WHERE/JOIN columns. If not, I add one. If yes, I check if the data type matches (implicit conversions kill seeks) or if a function is wrapped around the indexed column (also kills seeks).

Golden rule that kills Index Seeks:

-- BAD: function on indexed column prevents seek
WHERE YEAR(OrderDate) = 2024

-- GOOD: use range instead
WHERE OrderDate >= '2024-01-01' AND OrderDate < '2025-01-01'

followUps.question: What causes a Table Scan even when an index exists?
What is a key lookup and when does it happen?
How do you read a SQL Server execution plan?

questions.tip: Functions on indexed columns prevent seeks. LIKE with leading wildcard prevents seeks. Check logical reads with SET STATISTICS IO ON.

questions.question: How do you reseed an identity column in SQL Server? What is DBCC CHECKIDENT?
questions.answer:
DBCC CHECKIDENT is the T-SQL command to check or reset (reseed) the current identity value of a table.

Check current identity value:

DBCC CHECKIDENT ('Orders', NORESEED);
-- Output: Checking identity information: current identity value '150', current column value '150'.

Reseed to a specific value:

-- Next insert will use 101 (seed + 1)
DBCC CHECKIDENT ('Orders', RESEED, 100);

-- Reset to 0 (next insert gets ID = 1)
DBCC CHECKIDENT ('Orders', RESEED, 0);

-- After TRUNCATE, identity is automatically reset — but after DELETE it's not
TRUNCATE TABLE Orders;  -- resets identity to seed value
DELETE FROM Orders;     -- does NOT reset identity
DBCC CHECKIDENT ('Orders', RESEED, 0);  -- manually reset after DELETE

Real scenario where I've used this:

In staging/test environments, after clearing test data with DELETE (not TRUNCATE, because of FK constraints), the identity values kept climbing — IDs like 50000 in a test table with 10 rows. I use DBCC CHECKIDENT to reset it to something sensible.

-- Safe pattern for test data cleanup:
DELETE FROM OrderItems;  -- child first (FK constraint)
DELETE FROM Orders;      -- parent
DBCC CHECKIDENT ('OrderItems', RESEED, 0);
DBCC CHECKIDENT ('Orders', RESEED, 0);

RESEED with value vs without:

-- RESEED with no value: resets to max existing value in the table
-- Useful if you manually inserted rows with SET IDENTITY_INSERT ON
DBCC CHECKIDENT ('Orders', RESEED);  -- no value = sync to max row

One gotcha: if you reseed to a value lower than existing rows, the next INSERT will skip to one above the current max (SQL Server 2014+ behavior) — so always RESEED to at least max existing ID to avoid PK violations.

followUps.question: What is the difference between TRUNCATE and DELETE regarding identity reset?
What is SET IDENTITY_INSERT ON and when do you use it?
Can you have a non-integer identity column?

questions.tip: TRUNCATE resets identity automatically. DELETE does not — use DBCC CHECKIDENT to reset manually.

questions.question: What are the types of SQL Server backups? Explain Full, Differential, and Transaction Log backups.
questions.answer:
SQL Server has three core backup types, and they work together in a strategy — you typically don't use just one.

1. Full Backup

A complete snapshot of the entire database — all data and enough of the transaction log to bring the database to a consistent state at the point in time of the backup.

BACKUP DATABASE InterviewPrep 
TO DISK = 'D:\Backups\InterviewPrep_Full_20240523.bak'
WITH COMPRESSION, STATS = 10;
  • Largest in size, slowest
  • Self-contained — can restore from just this file
  • Typically done weekly (or daily for critical DBs)

2. Differential Backup

Captures only the pages changed since the last Full backup. Much faster and smaller than another full backup.

BACKUP DATABASE InterviewPrep
TO DISK = 'D:\Backups\InterviewPrep_Diff_20240523.bak'
WITH DIFFERENTIAL, COMPRESSION;
  • Grows larger over time until the next full backup
  • To restore: Full + latest Differential
  • Typically done daily or every few hours

3. Transaction Log Backup

Backs up the transaction log — all changes since the last log backup. Enables point-in-time recovery. Only works when Recovery Model is Full or Bulk-Logged.

BACKUP LOG InterviewPrep
TO DISK = 'D:\Backups\InterviewPrep_Log_20240523_14h.bak'
WITH COMPRESSION;
  • Smallest and fastest
  • Also truncates the inactive portion of the log (prevents log file bloat)
  • To restore: Full + last Differential + all Log backups since the Differential
  • Typically done every 15-60 minutes in production

Typical production backup strategy:

-- Sunday: Full backup
-- Mon-Sat: Differential backup (nightly)
-- Every 30 min: Transaction Log backup

-- To restore to Tuesday 2:45pm:
-- 1. Restore Sunday Full (NORECOVERY)
-- 2. Restore Monday night Differential (NORECOVERY)
-- 3. Restore all log backups from Monday night through Tuesday 2:45pm (RECOVERY at end)

In my experience, most teams underestimate how critical log backups are. I had a scenario where we needed to restore to a specific minute before accidental bulk deletes. Transaction log backups saved us — we recovered to the exact minute before the mistake.

followUps.question: What is point-in-time recovery and when is it needed?
What is Copy-Only backup?
What recovery model allows transaction log backups?

questions.tip: Remember the restore chain: Full → Differential → Log backups. You need all of them in sequence.

questions.question: What are the High Availability solutions in SQL Server? Explain Mirroring, Log Shipping, Replication, and Always On.
questions.answer:
SQL Server provides several HA (High Availability) and DR (Disaster Recovery) solutions. Here's how they compare — this comes up in senior-level interviews:

1. Always On Availability Groups (Modern — preferred)

One primary replica + up to 8 secondary replicas. Secondaries can be readable. Automatic failover in seconds.

  • ✅ Automatic failover, readable secondaries, offload reporting queries
  • ✅ Synchronous (zero data loss) or Asynchronous (geo-redundancy)
  • ❌ Requires Windows Server Failover Clustering (WSFC) or Domain-independent AG
  • Introduced in SQL Server 2012, enhanced ever since

2. Database Mirroring (Deprecated in 2012, removed in 2022)

One principal + one mirror, with optional witness for automatic failover. Sends transaction log records to mirror in real-time.

  • High Safety mode: synchronous (zero data loss, automatic failover with witness)
  • High Performance mode: asynchronous (some data loss possible)
  • Mirror is not readable — no offloading
  • ❌ Deprecated — don't use in new systems, mention Always On instead

3. Log Shipping

Automates: backup transaction logs on primary → copy to standby server → restore on standby. Low-cost DR solution.

-- What happens under the hood (automated jobs):
-- Primary:  BACKUP LOG every 15 minutes → network share
-- Standby:  Copy job picks up the file
-- Standby:  Restore job applies it (WITH STANDBY or NORECOVERY)
  • ✅ Simple, low cost, works across versions, standby can be readable (WITH STANDBY)
  • ❌ Manual failover, data loss = last log backup interval (15-30 min typically)
  • Good for: budget-conscious DR where some data loss is acceptable

4. Replication

Not really HA — it's data distribution. Copies data changes from publisher to subscriber(s).

  • Snapshot: full copy periodically
  • Transactional: near real-time changes (used for read scale-out)
  • Merge: bidirectional sync (mobile/disconnected scenarios)
  • Used for: reporting databases, distributing data to multiple locations, read offloading
  • ❌ Not a failover solution — subscriber can have subset of data

Quick comparison table:

SolutionFailoverData LossReadable Secondary
Always On AGAuto (seconds)Zero (sync)Yes
MirroringAuto (with witness)Zero (sync)No
Log ShippingManualMinutesYes (STANDBY)
ReplicationN/AN/AYes (subscriber)

In any new project today, I recommend Always On AGs. Log shipping is a good cheap fallback when you can't afford the WSFC infrastructure.

followUps.question: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous availability groups?
What is Failover Cluster Instance (FCI) vs Availability Groups?
What is RTO and RPO in disaster recovery?

questions.tip: Always On AGs are the modern answer. Mirroring is deprecated. Log Shipping is cheap but manual failover.

questions.question: What are DCL commands in SQL? Explain GRANT, DENY, and REVOKE.
questions.answer:
DCL stands for Data Control Language. It controls who can access and manipulate database objects.

GRANT — Give permissions

-- Grant SELECT on a table to a user
GRANT SELECT ON dbo.Employees TO [AppUser];

-- Grant multiple permissions
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE ON dbo.Orders TO [AppUser];

-- Grant EXECUTE on a stored procedure
GRANT EXECUTE ON dbo.uspGetOrderSummary TO [ReportingUser];

-- Grant to a role (better practice — manage roles, not individual users)
GRANT SELECT ON dbo.Products TO [ReadOnlyRole];

DENY — Explicitly block permissions (overrides GRANT)

-- Even if AppUser is in a role that has DELETE, this prevents it
DENY DELETE ON dbo.Employees TO [AppUser];

-- DENY is the strongest — it overrides any GRANT from role membership

REVOKE — Remove a previously granted or denied permission

-- Remove the SELECT grant (user loses permission if not granted elsewhere)
REVOKE SELECT ON dbo.Employees FROM [AppUser];

-- Revoke a DENY (removes the explicit deny, but doesn't grant access)
REVOKE DELETE ON dbo.Employees FROM [AppUser];

Permission precedence:

DENY > GRANT always. If a user is in Role A (which has GRANT SELECT) and you DENY SELECT to that user directly, they cannot SELECT — the DENY wins.

Practical approach I use:

-- Create roles, not per-user grants
CREATE ROLE ReadOnlyRole;
CREATE ROLE AppRole;

GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA::dbo TO ReadOnlyRole;
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE ON SCHEMA::dbo TO AppRole;
GRANT EXECUTE ON SCHEMA::dbo TO AppRole;

-- Assign users to roles
ALTER ROLE AppRole ADD MEMBER [WebAppUser];
ALTER ROLE ReadOnlyRole ADD MEMBER [ReportingUser];

In real-world apps, I always use least privilege — the web app service account only gets SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on specific tables and EXECUTE on stored procs. Never db_owner or sa in production.

followUps.question: What is the difference between REVOKE and DENY?
What are database roles vs server roles in SQL Server?
What is the principle of least privilege in database security?

questions.tip: DENY always wins over GRANT — even if the user gets GRANT through a role, an explicit DENY blocks them.

questions.question: What is Fill Factor in SQL Server? What is the default value and when do you change it?
questions.answer:
Fill Factor determines how full SQL Server fills index pages when creating or rebuilding an index. It's expressed as a percentage from 1–100.

Default Fill Factor

Server-level default is 0, which is treated the same as 100% — pages are filled completely during creation/rebuild.

-- Check server-level default
EXEC sp_configure 'fill factor (%)';
-- Typically returns 0 (= 100% full)

-- Check fill factor on a specific index
SELECT name, fill_factor 
FROM sys.indexes 
WHERE object_id = OBJECT_ID('Orders');

Why would you NOT fill pages 100%?

When pages are 100% full and a new row needs to be inserted, SQL Server must perform a page split — it moves half the data to a new page. Page splits:

  • Cause fragmentation
  • Are slow (I/O heavy)
  • Happen frequently on tables with heavy INSERT/UPDATE activity

Setting Fill Factor — Leave room for growth

-- Create index with 80% fill factor (leaves 20% free space)
CREATE INDEX IX_Orders_CustomerId ON Orders(CustomerId)
WITH (FILLFACTOR = 80);

-- Rebuild with different fill factor
ALTER INDEX IX_Orders_CustomerId ON Orders
REBUILD WITH (FILLFACTOR = 80);

Guidelines from real experience:

ScenarioFill FactorWhy
Read-heavy, rarely updated95-100%Minimize I/O, no splits needed
Moderate INSERT/UPDATE activity80-90%Balance between space and splits
Heavy random inserts (e.g., GUID PKs)70-80%Lots of page splits without free space
Monotonically increasing PK (INT IDENTITY)95-100%Inserts always at end, no mid-page splits

One important note: Fill Factor only applies at rebuild/creation time. As data comes in, pages fill up until the next rebuild. That's why regular index maintenance (weekly/monthly rebuilds) is part of any production DBA routine.

-- Maintain indexes (rebuild if fragmentation > 30%, reorganize if 10-30%)
ALTER INDEX ALL ON Orders REBUILD;  -- reapplies the fill factor

followUps.question: What is the difference between REBUILD and REORGANIZE for indexes?
What is a page split and why is it bad?
How does Fill Factor relate to index fragmentation?

questions.tip: Fill Factor 0 = 100% full. Lower value = more free space = fewer page splits = less fragmentation over time.

questions.question: What are the Recovery Models in SQL Server? Explain Simple, Full, and Bulk-Logged.
questions.answer:
The Recovery Model controls how SQL Server manages the transaction log — specifically how much log is retained and what kinds of recovery are possible.

1. Simple Recovery Model

The transaction log is automatically truncated after each checkpoint. No transaction log backups possible.

ALTER DATABASE MyDb SET RECOVERY SIMPLE;
  • ✅ Log file stays small, auto-managed
  • ✅ No log backup overhead
  • ❌ Can only restore to last Full or Differential backup
  • ❌ No point-in-time recovery
  • Use for: development databases, data warehouses with full nightly backups, non-critical systems

2. Full Recovery Model

Every transaction is fully logged and log is retained until you explicitly back it up.

ALTER DATABASE MyDb SET RECOVERY FULL;
  • ✅ Point-in-time recovery (restore to any specific minute)
  • ✅ Transaction log backups possible
  • ❌ Log file grows until backed up — must have regular log backup jobs
  • Use for: production OLTP systems, any database where you can't afford to lose more than minutes of data
-- Typical job setup under Full recovery:
-- Every 30 min: BACKUP LOG MyDb TO DISK = '...'
-- This truncates the inactive log AND creates the log backup file

3. Bulk-Logged Recovery Model

Like Full, but minimally logs certain bulk operations (BULK INSERT, SELECT INTO, index rebuilds). Designed for temporary use during large data loads.

-- Pattern: switch to Bulk-Logged during load
ALTER DATABASE MyDb SET RECOVERY BULK_LOGGED;

-- Run your bulk operation
BULK INSERT Orders FROM 'C:\data\orders.csv' WITH (FIELDTERMINATOR=',');
-- SELECT INTO, CREATE INDEX also benefit here

-- Switch back after load
ALTER DATABASE MyDb SET RECOVERY FULL;
BACKUP LOG MyDb TO DISK = 'D:\Backups\AfterBulkLoad.bak';
  • ✅ Dramatically faster for bulk operations
  • ✅ Much smaller log growth during bulk loads
  • ❌ Log backup containing bulk operation cannot be used for point-in-time recovery
  • Use for: ETL windows, initial data loads, periodic large batch operations

Which one to choose in production?

OLTP production → Full always. Simple for dev/test. Bulk-Logged temporarily during large ETL jobs then switch back to Full immediately after and take a log backup.

followUps.question: What happens to the transaction log if I forget to take log backups under Full Recovery?
Can you switch between recovery models? What are the implications?
What is a log chain and why does it matter for restore?

questions.tip: Under Full Recovery, if you don't take regular log backups, the log file grows forever — set up a SQL Agent job.

questions.question: What are the types of SQL commands? Explain DDL, DML, DQL, DCL, and TCL.
questions.answer:
SQL commands are grouped into five categories based on what they do. This is a very common first SQL interview question.

1. DDL — Data Definition Language

Defines and modifies the structure of database objects. Changes are auto-committed.

CREATE TABLE Orders (OrderId INT PRIMARY KEY, Total DECIMAL(10,2));
ALTER  TABLE Orders ADD Column ShippedDate DATE;
DROP   TABLE TempOrders;
TRUNCATE TABLE Orders;  -- also DDL (structure-level operation)
RENAME TABLE OldOrders TO ArchivedOrders;

2. DML — Data Manipulation Language

Manipulates the data inside tables. Changes can be rolled back (within a transaction).

INSERT INTO Orders (Total) VALUES (250.00);
UPDATE Orders SET Total = 300.00 WHERE OrderId = 1;
DELETE FROM Orders WHERE OrderId = 1;
MERGE INTO Orders AS target  -- upsert (SQL Server)
    USING @source AS source ON target.OrderId = source.OrderId
    WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE ...
    WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ...;

3. DQL — Data Query Language

Queries data. Some classify SELECT under DML, but many treat it separately.

SELECT OrderId, Total FROM Orders WHERE Status = 'pending' ORDER BY Total DESC;

4. DCL — Data Control Language

Controls access and permissions.

GRANT SELECT, INSERT ON Orders TO AppUser;
DENY  DELETE ON Orders TO ReadOnlyUser;
REVOKE INSERT ON Orders FROM AppUser;

5. TCL — Transaction Control Language

Manages transactions — commits or rolls back DML changes.

BEGIN TRANSACTION;
    UPDATE Accounts SET Balance = Balance - 1000 WHERE AccountId = 1;
    UPDATE Accounts SET Balance = Balance + 1000 WHERE AccountId = 2;
COMMIT;   -- save changes

-- or
ROLLBACK; -- undo all changes in this transaction

SAVEPOINT sp1;           -- mark a point within transaction
ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT sp1;  -- partial rollback

Quick memory trick:

  • DDL = Create/Alter/Drop structure
  • DML = Insert/Update/Delete data
  • DQL = Select read data
  • DCL = Grant/Deny/Revoke access
  • TCL = Commit/Rollback transactions

followUps.question: Why is TRUNCATE a DDL command if it removes data?
Can you roll back a DDL statement?
What is the difference between DELETE and TRUNCATE in terms of transaction logging?

questions.tip: DDL auto-commits (can't rollback). DML is transactional (can rollback). TRUNCATE is DDL so it's not rollbackable in most RDBMS.

questions.question: What is a CASE statement in SQL? Explain SIMPLE vs SEARCHED CASE with examples.
questions.answer:
The CASE statement adds conditional logic to SQL queries — it's essentially an if-else block inside a SELECT, WHERE, or ORDER BY clause.

1. Simple CASE — compare one expression to multiple values

SELECT 
    OrderId,
    Status,
    CASE Status
        WHEN 'pending'   THEN 'Awaiting Processing'
        WHEN 'shipped'   THEN 'On the Way'
        WHEN 'delivered' THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN 'cancelled' THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE 'Unknown'
    END AS StatusDescription
FROM Orders;

2. Searched CASE — evaluate different conditions

SELECT 
    ProductId,
    Price,
    CASE 
        WHEN Price < 100    THEN 'Budget'
        WHEN Price < 500    THEN 'Mid-Range'
        WHEN Price < 2000   THEN 'Premium'
        ELSE 'Luxury'
    END AS PriceCategory,
    CASE 
        WHEN Stock = 0      THEN 'Out of Stock'
        WHEN Stock < 10     THEN 'Low Stock'
        ELSE 'In Stock'
    END AS StockStatus
FROM Products;

CASE in aggregate functions — pivot-style reporting:

-- Count orders by status in a single row
SELECT 
    COUNT(CASE WHEN Status = 'pending'   THEN 1 END) AS PendingCount,
    COUNT(CASE WHEN Status = 'shipped'   THEN 1 END) AS ShippedCount,
    COUNT(CASE WHEN Status = 'delivered' THEN 1 END) AS DeliveredCount,
    SUM(CASE WHEN Status = 'delivered'   THEN Total ELSE 0 END) AS DeliveredRevenue
FROM Orders;

CASE in ORDER BY — custom sort:

-- Prioritize urgent orders first
SELECT * FROM Orders
ORDER BY 
    CASE Priority
        WHEN 'urgent'  THEN 1
        WHEN 'high'    THEN 2
        WHEN 'normal'  THEN 3
        ELSE 4
    END,
    CreatedDate ASC;

CASE in UPDATE:

-- Apply different discounts based on customer tier
UPDATE Orders
SET DiscountAmount = Total * 
    CASE CustomerTier
        WHEN 'Gold'   THEN 0.20
        WHEN 'Silver' THEN 0.10
        WHEN 'Bronze' THEN 0.05
        ELSE 0
    END
WHERE Status = 'pending';

I use CASE expressions frequently in reporting queries and stored procedures. The pivot-style aggregation pattern (COUNT + CASE) is very powerful for building summary dashboards without pivot tables.

followUps.question: What is the difference between CASE and IIF() in SQL Server?
Can you use CASE in a WHERE clause?
How do you handle NULLs in CASE expressions?

questions.tip: CASE is evaluated left to right — first matching WHEN wins. NULLs don't match with = in CASE; use WHEN column IS NULL.

questions.question: What is a Self Join in SQL? When and why would you use one?
questions.answer:
A self join is when a table is joined to itself. You use it when rows in the same table have a hierarchical or peer relationship — for example, an Employee table where each row has a ManagerId that points to another row in the same table.

Classic example — Employee and Manager in same table:

CREATE TABLE Employee (
    EmployeeId INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Name       NVARCHAR(100),
    ManagerId  INT NULL,  -- FK to same table (NULL = top of hierarchy)
    Department NVARCHAR(50)
);

-- Self join to get employee + their manager's name
SELECT 
    e.EmployeeId,
    e.Name          AS EmployeeName,
    m.Name          AS ManagerName,
    e.Department
FROM Employee e
LEFT JOIN Employee m ON e.ManagerId = m.EmployeeId;
-- LEFT JOIN ensures employees with no manager (CEO) are still returned

-- Result:
-- EmployeeId  EmployeeName  ManagerName   Department
-- 1           Alice         NULL          Engineering   (Alice is CEO)
-- 2           Bob           Alice         Engineering
-- 3           Carol         Alice         Sales
-- 4           Dave          Bob           Engineering

Find all employees who earn more than their manager:

SELECT 
    e.Name          AS Employee,
    e.Salary        AS EmployeeSalary,
    m.Name          AS Manager,
    m.Salary        AS ManagerSalary
FROM Employee e
INNER JOIN Employee m ON e.ManagerId = m.EmployeeId
WHERE e.Salary > m.Salary;

Another use case — finding duplicates or related records:

-- Find customers in the same city
SELECT 
    c1.Name AS Customer1,
    c2.Name AS Customer2,
    c1.City
FROM Customers c1
INNER JOIN Customers c2 
    ON c1.City = c2.City 
    AND c1.CustomerId < c2.CustomerId  -- avoid duplicates and self-matches
ORDER BY c1.City;

Key point — always use aliases:

You must alias the table when self-joining because SQL can't tell which instance of the table you mean. Typically I use e for employee and m for manager, or p and c for parent and child.

In EF Core, you'd model this as a navigation property pointing to the same entity, and EF generates the self-join automatically in the SQL it produces.

followUps.question: How would you model a self-referential relationship in EF Core?
What is the difference between a self join and a recursive CTE for hierarchical data?
How do you avoid duplicate rows in a self join?

questions.tip: Always use aliases in self joins. Use LEFT JOIN to include top-level records (like CEO with no manager). Recursive CTEs are better for deep hierarchies.

questions.question: What is a Cursor in SQL Server? When should you use one and when should you avoid it?
questions.answer:
A cursor is a database object that allows you to process query results row-by-row, like a loop in application code. Think of it as a pointer that moves through a result set one row at a time.

Basic cursor syntax:

DECLARE @EmployeeId INT, @Name NVARCHAR(100), @Salary DECIMAL(10,2);

-- Declare the cursor
DECLARE emp_cursor CURSOR FOR
    SELECT EmployeeId, Name, Salary FROM Employees WHERE Department = 'Sales';

-- Open it (executes the query)
OPEN emp_cursor;

-- Fetch first row
FETCH NEXT FROM emp_cursor INTO @EmployeeId, @Name, @Salary;

-- Loop through rows
WHILE @@FETCH_STATUS = 0
BEGIN
    -- Process each row
    PRINT 'Processing: ' + @Name + ' - Salary: ' + CAST(@Salary AS VARCHAR);
    
    -- Do something with this row...
    
    -- Fetch next row
    FETCH NEXT FROM emp_cursor INTO @EmployeeId, @Name, @Salary;
END;

-- Always clean up
CLOSE emp_cursor;
DEALLOCATE emp_cursor;

When cursors are actually justified:

  • Row-by-row processing that truly can't be set-based (rare)
  • Calling a stored procedure for each row
  • Complex calculations that depend on previous row results
  • Administrative/maintenance tasks that must be logged per-row

⚠️ When to AVOID cursors (almost always):

Cursors are slow because SQL Server is optimized for set-based operations, not row-by-row iteration. A cursor on a million rows might take minutes; the equivalent set-based UPDATE takes seconds.

-- BAD: Cursor to give 10% raise to all Sales employees
DECLARE @Id INT
DECLARE c CURSOR FOR SELECT EmployeeId FROM Employees WHERE Department = 'Sales'
OPEN c
FETCH NEXT FROM c INTO @Id
WHILE @@FETCH_STATUS = 0
BEGIN
    UPDATE Employees SET Salary = Salary * 1.10 WHERE EmployeeId = @Id
    FETCH NEXT FROM c INTO @Id
END
CLOSE c; DEALLOCATE c;

-- GOOD: Set-based — one statement, much faster
UPDATE Employees 
SET Salary = Salary * 1.10 
WHERE Department = 'Sales';

Cursor alternatives I use instead:

  • WHILE loop with MIN() to iterate — slightly faster than cursor
  • Set-based UPDATE/INSERT/MERGE for bulk changes
  • Recursive CTEs for hierarchical processing
  • Application-side processing — pull data to C#, process in memory, write back

My general rule: if I find myself reaching for a cursor, I take 5 minutes to think about a set-based alternative first. 9 out of 10 times, one exists.

followUps.question: What is the difference between STATIC, DYNAMIC, FAST_FORWARD cursor types?
How do you optimize a cursor if you absolutely must use one?
What is a WHILE loop alternative to cursors?

questions.tip: FAST_FORWARD cursors are the fastest type if you must use one — read-only, forward-only. But set-based is almost always better.

questions.question: What is the COALESCE() function in SQL Server? How is it different from ISNULL()?
questions.answer:
Both handle NULL substitution, but they differ in portability, number of arguments, and how SQL Server evaluates them.

COALESCE() — ANSI SQL standard. Returns the first non-NULL expression from a list of any number of arguments. Can accept multiple values.

ISNULL() — SQL Server-specific. Accepts exactly two arguments. Slightly faster in SQL Server due to being a native function.

-- COALESCE: returns first non-NULL value from the list
SELECT COALESCE(NULL, NULL, 'Third', 'Fourth') -- returns 'Third'

-- ISNULL: only two arguments
SELECT ISNULL(NULL, 'Fallback') -- returns 'Fallback'

-- Practical example: show nickname if available, otherwise full name
SELECT 
    COALESCE(NickName, FirstName + ' ' + LastName, 'Unknown') AS DisplayName
FROM Employees;

-- Phone number priority: work > mobile > home
SELECT 
    EmployeeId,
    COALESCE(WorkPhone, MobilePhone, HomePhone, 'No phone') AS ContactNumber
FROM Employees;

-- ISNULL equivalent — only handles single fallback
SELECT ISNULL(WorkPhone, 'No phone') AS ContactNumber FROM Employees;

-- Key difference: data type inference
-- COALESCE returns type based on data type precedence of all args
-- ISNULL returns the data type of the first argument (can truncate!)
DECLARE @x NCHAR(3) = NULL;
SELECT ISNULL(@x, 'Fallback');    -- returns 'Fal' (truncated to NCHAR(3)!)
SELECT COALESCE(@x, 'Fallback'); -- returns 'Fallback' (uses higher precedence type)

-- Common use in SUM with NULLs
SELECT SUM(COALESCE(Discount, 0)) AS TotalDiscount FROM Orders;
-- Same as: SUM(ISNULL(Discount, 0))

-- COALESCE with NULL check in WHERE
SELECT * FROM Products
WHERE COALESCE(DiscontinuedDate, '9999-12-31') > GETDATE();

My rule: Use COALESCE for portability and when you need multiple fallback values. Use ISNULL when performance is critical in SQL Server and you have exactly two values — it's marginally faster. Watch the data type truncation trap with ISNULL.

followUps.question: What is the data type truncation issue with ISNULL?
Can COALESCE cause issues with non-deterministic functions being evaluated multiple times?
How does NULLIF() differ from COALESCE?

questions.tip: COALESCE = ANSI standard, multiple args, returns first non-NULL. ISNULL = SQL Server only, 2 args, slightly faster. Watch: ISNULL uses the first argument's data type (can truncate). Use COALESCE for portability.

questions.question: What is the difference between DBMS and RDBMS?
questions.answer:
A fundamental theory question. The key distinction is that RDBMS enforces relationships between tables using foreign keys and referential integrity — which a basic DBMS doesn't do.

FeatureDBMSRDBMS
Full formDatabase Management SystemRelational DBMS
Data storageFiles, hierarchical, networkTables (rows and columns)
RelationshipsNo enforced relationshipsForeign keys enforce referential integrity
KeysNo concept of primary keyPrimary key, foreign key, unique key
ACIDNot necessarily ACIDFull ACID compliance
NormalizationNot supportedSupported (1NF, 2NF, 3NF)
ExamplesXML DBMS, file systems, IMSSQL Server, MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle
Query languageNo standard query languageSQL (Structured Query Language)
-- RDBMS enforces relationships with foreign keys
CREATE TABLE Customers (
    CustomerId INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Name NVARCHAR(100)
);

CREATE TABLE Orders (
    OrderId INT PRIMARY KEY,
    CustomerId INT NOT NULL,
    OrderDate DATETIME,
    FOREIGN KEY (CustomerId) REFERENCES Customers(CustomerId)
    -- RDBMS prevents inserting an order for non-existent customer
    -- A basic DBMS would not enforce this!
);

-- Referential integrity in action
INSERT INTO Orders VALUES (1, 999, GETDATE()); -- ERROR if CustomerId 999 doesn't exist

In practice, everything we work with as .NET developers — SQL Server, MySQL, PostgreSQL — is an RDBMS. The distinction matters in interviews as a theory question, and it's worth noting that modern NoSQL databases like MongoDB are technically DBMS (not RDBMS) because they don't enforce relational constraints.

followUps.question: Is MongoDB a DBMS or RDBMS?
What are the Codd's 12 rules for RDBMS?
Can you use an RDBMS without normalizing your data?

questions.tip: DBMS = manages data files (no enforced relationships). RDBMS = tables + foreign keys + ACID + SQL. All SQL Server/MySQL/PostgreSQL are RDBMS. NoSQL (MongoDB, Redis) are DBMS variants. The key difference: referential integrity enforcement.
